event: Early hominin and Paleolithic human presence is evidenced by stone tools found in the Karatau Mountains of southern Kazakhstan.
The Botai culture flourishes on the northern steppe, where some scholars argue horses were first domesticated.
Bronze Age Andronovo culture spreads across the steppe, leaving extensive kurgan burials and metalworking sites.
Saka (Scythian) nomadic tribes dominate the territory, producing the renowned gold-adorned 'Golden Man' burial at Issyk.
Queen Tomyris of the Massagetae defeats and kills the Persian king Cyrus the Great near the Syr Darya, according to Herodotus.
Alexander the Great campaigns near the Syr Darya, founding Alexandria Eschate at the edge of the Central Asian steppe.
The Kangju and Wusun nomadic confederations control the river valleys and steppe of present-day Kazakhstan.
The Turkic Khaganate is established, bringing the region under nomadic Turkic rule and spreading Turkic languages.
Arab Abbasid forces defeat Tang Chinese armies at the Battle of Talas, opening Central Asia to Islam and paper-making.
The Karluk Turks establish dominance in Semirechye, becoming a leading power in the western steppe.
The Oghuz Turks and other nomadic groups consolidate power across the western Kazakh steppe and Aral region.
The Karakhanid Khanate rises, the first Turkic dynasty to adopt Islam as a state religion in Central Asia.
Genghis Khan's Mongol armies sack Otrar and other Silk Road cities, devastating the region during the conquest of Khwarazm.
Following Genghis Khan's death, the lands become part of the Mongol successor states, mainly the Golden Horde and Chagatai Khanate.
Timur (Tamerlane) campaigns across the steppe, defeating the Golden Horde's Tokhtamysh at the Battle of the Kondurcha River.
Sultans Janibek and Kerei break away from the Uzbek Khanate to found the Kazakh Khanate, a foundational moment for Kazakh statehood.
Khan Kasym consolidates and expands the Kazakh Khanate, greatly increasing its territory and population.
The Dzungar (Oirat) Khanate forms to the east, beginning more than a century of devastating wars against the Kazakhs.
After internal strife the Kazakh Khanate effectively fragments into three jüz (hordes): the Great, Middle, and Lesser.
The 'Great Disaster' (Aqtaban Shubyryndy) begins as massive Dzungar invasions force mass Kazakh flight and famine.
Kazakh forces under Abulkhair Khan win a major victory over the Dzungars at the Battle of Anyrakai.
Abulkhair Khan of the Lesser Jüz accepts Russian protection, beginning the gradual Russian colonization of the Kazakh lands.
Russia abolishes the khan's authority in the Middle Jüz, replacing traditional rule with imperial administrative reforms.
Kenesary Kasymov launches a major decade-long uprising against Russian rule, the last great Kazakh national revolt.
Russians found the fortress of Verny (later Almaty), strengthening imperial control over Semirechye.
Russia formally annexes the remaining Kazakh territories, dividing them into administrative oblasts of the empire.
Russian peasant resettlement onto the steppe accelerates, displacing nomadic Kazakhs from traditional grazing lands.
The Central Asian Revolt erupts against tsarist conscription, met with brutal repression and the flight of many Kazakhs to China.
Kazakh intellectuals form the autonomous Alash Orda government amid the Russian Revolution and ensuing civil war.
The Kirghiz (Kazakh) Autonomous Soviet Socialist Republic is established within Soviet Russia.
The republic is renamed the Kazakh ASSR, more accurately reflecting the identity of its titular people.
Forced Soviet collectivization and sedentarization devastate Kazakh herders, triggering a catastrophic famine.
The Goloshchekin famine kills well over a million Kazakhs, drastically reducing the ethnic Kazakh population.
Kazakhstan is elevated to a full Soviet Socialist Republic of the USSR.
During World War II, factories and deported peoples are relocated to Kazakhstan, which supplies troops and resources.
The Soviet Union detonates its first atomic bomb at the Semipalatinsk Test Site, beginning decades of nuclear testing.
Khrushchev's Virgin Lands Campaign plows vast areas of northern steppe for grain, transforming the economy and demographics.
The Baikonur Cosmodrome is established, later launching Sputnik and Yuri Gagarin into space.
The Jeltoqsan protests erupt in Almaty against Moscow's appointment of a non-Kazakh party leader, an early sign of unrest.
Nursultan Nazarbayev becomes head of the Kazakh Communist Party as the Nevada-Semipalatinsk anti-nuclear movement gains force.
Kazakhstan declares independence on 16 December as the Soviet Union collapses, with Nazarbayev as president.
Kazakhstan is admitted to the United Nations on 2 March as a sovereign state.
Kazakhstan introduces its own currency, the tenge, replacing the Soviet ruble.
Kazakhstan gives up the nuclear weapons inherited from the USSR, becoming a non-nuclear state under the NPT.
The capital is moved from Almaty to Akmola, soon renamed Astana, in the north of the country.
Major foreign-led development of the giant Tengiz and later Kashagan oil fields fuels rapid economic growth.
Kazakhstan becomes the first post-Soviet and Muslim-majority state to chair the OSCE, hosting a summit in Astana.
Astana hosts Expo 2017, themed 'Future Energy', showcasing the country on the world stage.
Nazarbayev resigns after nearly three decades in power; Kassym-Jomart Tokayev becomes president and the capital is renamed Nur-Sultan.
Deadly 'Bloody January' protests over fuel prices lead to a CSTO intervention, a government reshuffle, and the capital reverting to Astana.
